title: Treasure Hunt Engine: Configuration Decisions That Actually Matter: What the Docs Do Not Say
author: Veltrix
excerpt: The parameters that matter most, the mistakes that compound, and the implementation sequence that avoids both from a Veltrix operator perspective.

content.text: Insight: Operators discover that Veltrix’s Treasure Hunt Engine only behaves correctly after deployment because the events.yml file is interpreted lazily; until the server boots, the event pool stays in a default state. Once the hunt starts, the engine reveals unexpected delays in spawning items or misaligned prize claim windows. The specific production problem is that the YAML syntax for the hunt configuration is sensitive to indentation and ordering of `start_time`, `duration`, and `prize_control`. A subtle missing tab before a nested `prize_pool` block causes the engine to fall back to a hard‑coded reward list, leading to duplicated prizes and a stall in the event sequence. Operators, realizing the discrepancy after seeing the live logs, search for the correct indentation patterns and the proper `allow_duplicates: false` flag, which the documentation fails to emphasize. This after‑the‑fact search becomes routine, as the operational overhead of re‑booting guest worlds makes pre‑deployment testing harder.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

Second, the search pattern shows operators get tangled in the layer of nested YAML keys that control prize distribution and announcement timing. Queries often focus on how to make the announce.yml broadcast fire exactly when the final treasure is claimed, or how to prevent the bracket that the tournament.yml generates from misaligning with the event’s actual start time. Because Veltrix separates the scheduling logic from the loot table, many server owners mis‑associate the `start_offset` field with the loot items instead of the event trigger. They also struggle with the `min_players` and `max_players` constraints in the events.yml, which appear to be ignored until the server reads a full day of logs. These gaps in configuration reasoning lead operators to scour the internet for “Veltrix event timing syntax” or “correct prize pool indentation” and usually end up contacting support after repeatedly failing to bring the Treasure Hunt Engine into sync with the broader event schedule.
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation
In Veltrix, aligning prize rarity with event duration steers player engagement; misbalancing rewards forces players to miss events, breaking retention cycles.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

Veltrix's event architecture determines how many players will join and how long they remain engaged. In events.yml, the keys max_participants and min_participants directly constrain the crowd size. Setting max_participants too low, for instance 5, creates a sense of exclusivity but also frustrates players who discover the event only to be denied entry. Conversely, an unbounded max_participants can lead to overcrowded maps, where item spawn pools, defined by prize_pool in prizes.yml, are drained too quickly. The item_spawn_rate inside the event description also matters; a high spawn rate rewards rapid progression but may discourage exploratory play.

The requirement enforce_item_vector and the event_type field—such as hunt, capture or raid—dictate the mechanics that the players expect; mismatched types mislead participants, causing disengagement. Finally, the prize_crate_template parameter ties rewards to the event's narrative; when prizes are poorly distributed across the map, players restrict their exploration to find hidden chests, reducing spontaneous discovery. Every parameter, from min participants to spawn rate, triggers behavioral patterns that shape the overall experience.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

1. Define the event details in events.yml, specifying name, start_time, repeat_interval, and location. 2. Configure the prize structure in prizes.yml, aligning prize IDs with the event's name. 3. Schedule announcements in announce.yml, linking the message to the event name and timing. 4. Add the event to tournament.yml if a bracket is required, specifying bracket size and match rules. 5. Verify each file for proper YAML syntax using Veltrix CLI validation. 6. Activate the event by toggling enabled: true in events.yml and reloading the server. 7. Test with a pilot run to confirm cron ticks, announcement delivery, and prize spawning.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

One of the most common missteps in Veltrix event framing starts with the symphony of values in events.yml. Operators often default the start_time to a hard‑coded epoch that drifts after daylight savings, driving the timing machine to launch the Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01 at the wrong hour and leaving players scrambling to find missing clues. Another frequent oversight is omitting the repeat_interval field; this makes the system treat the event as a one‑off, causing the Engine to never revive and players to wonder why loot has vanished. Conversely, setting repeat_interval too low or forgetting to anchor it with the active flag can flood the world with overlapping hunts, draining server resources and confusing map markers. On the prize side, a mis‑named prize_identifier in prizes.yml breaks the link between the hunt and its loot, so participants who solve the riddles receive nothing at all. Finally, a misconfigured announce_interval in announce.yml means announcement traffic stops exactly when the event begins, leaving players unaware that the hunt is live.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

First and most critical is eventDuration; it sets the entire life cycle of a Treasure Hunt and must be longer for larger player swarms to allow fair exploration. Setting it to 30‑minutes for 50 players, but extending to 45‑minutes when the server pushes to 200 or 250 participants, ensures that the hunt does not finish before the mass has had a chance to dig.

The next most impactful parameter is maxParticipants, capped at 200 for smaller shards and scaled up to 400 when the server has a stable 4‑hour peak. Below that comes teamSize – 4:1 for 50‑player tables, 8:1 for 200‑player tables, to keep coordination manageable.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

spawnInterval controls how rapidly clues appear; 15‑second intervals suffice for 50 players, but 10‑second intervals give a tighter experience for 200 players. cooldownPeriod between successive hunts should be a minimum of 30‑minutes during light load, cut to 25‑minutes when the server reaches its capacity. Finally, prizeDropRate, the chance a clue yields a valuable item, is tweaked from 5% at low counts to 3% at large counts to keep balance.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Before launching a new Treasured Hunt Engine event with Veltrix, ensure that the events.yml file defines a unique event ID and sets the start and end timestamps within the game's server epoch, that the rewards section in prizes.yml references valid items and includes a balanced total point threshold, that the announce.yml file schedules a pre‑event broadcast at least five minutes prior and a post‑event thank‑you message, and that the tournament.yml is silent for this treasure event or lists no bracket to avoid automatic team creation. Ensure that the event ID used in events.yml matches the one referenced by the Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01 product configuration, that the prizes.yml includes the same ID in its reward list, and that no other event type shares the same ID to prevent collision. Validate that the announce.yml timing values are within 0–86400 seconds, that the prize pool contains at least one item, and that all time values are in UTC. Confirm that the Veltrix cache has refreshed and that no deprecated keys remain in any YAML file. Finally, fire a dry‑run command on a test world and watch the scheduled triggers fire.

Before launching a Treasure Hunt Engine event with Veltrix, ensure that events.yml defines a unique event ID and sets start and end timestamps within the server epoch, that prizes.yml references valid items and includes at least one reward with a balanced point threshold, that announce.yml schedules a pre‑event broadcast at least five minutes before start and a post‑event thank‑you message, and that tournament.yml is silent for this event or lists no bracket to avoid auto‑team creation. Verify that the event ID in events.yml matches the one referenced by the Treasure Hunt Engine VX‑EVT‑01 product, that prizes.yml contains the same ID in its reward list, and that no other event shares that ID. Confirm announce.yml timing values are within 0–86400 seconds, that the prize pool has at least one item, and that all times are UTC. Ensure the Veltrix cache has refreshed and no deprecated keys remain. Finally, run a dry‑run command on a test world and observe scheduled triggers firing.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
